When Death Is Not the End
nytimes_articles.abstract:
The headlines shout our confusion: "Court to Decide Brain-Dead Baby's Fate";  "Medical Certainty, Legal Limbo"; "Hospital Ordered to Keep Brain-Dead Baby Alive." 

     Malcolm and Lois Scoon of Jamaica, Queens, whose 5-month-old baby, Mariah, has been declared "brain dead," have sought to keep her on artificial support even though she is legally a corpse. A State Health Department regulation requires physicians who declare someone dead to make "reasonable accommodation"  for those believing, for religious reasons, that death does not occur until the  heart stops. (The Scoons, born-again Christians, are under investigation for possibly causing the death of their baby.)

nytimes_articles.headline: And The Winner Is ...
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Citizens dismayed at the prospect of attending to a seemingly endless slog of primaries in the months ahead need no longer despair. Sure, we have South Carolina's primary today, and Georgia, Colorado, Rhode Island, Connecticut, Maine, Massachusetts, Vermont and Maryland this Tuesday. And then New York on Thursday, more contenders the following week, and on and on. By April, we stand  to be almost comatose with the sheer suspense of it all. 

      But that danger may be illusory. For, in fact, the Republican contest has now achieved a state of near-perfect negative equilibrium, one in which the media invariably cast each primary as a two-man race between the momentary leaders, whereupon voters evince their revulsion at that prospect by opting for  some third candidate altogether (compelled thus to pick between Bob Dole and Steve Forbes, New Hampshirites selected Pat Buchanan; offered the clear choice between Pat Buchanan and Bob Dole, Arizonans plopped for Steve Forbes; at this rate, even Lamar Alexander stands to win somewhere). The outcome can already be  predicted with such certainty as to render any further day-to-day vigilance entirely superfluous.

Wrong Again
nytimes_articles.abstract:
If bookies and stockbrokers were as wrong as often as political analysts and  commentators have been lately, they would be broke. Only major league baseball managers can fail so consistently and still find work. Consider all the speculation about winners and losers leading up to the Iowa caucuses and the New Hampshire and Arizona primaries. 

     On weekends, "Meet the Press," "Washington Week in Review," "This Week With David Brinkley," "Face the Nation," "The Capital Gang," "Evans and Novak," "The  McLaughlin Group" and "Media Watch," among others, offer a midway carnival of guessing games. Step right up and handicap the outcome. You don't have a clue? Then you can play. Fire away. Who knows, you may get lucky.

THEATER REVIEW;The Life and Art Of Clint Holmes
PUFFINS GALORE
nytimes_articles.abstract:
WHEN THE DIRECTOR OF PUBLIC programs at Harvard's Museum of Comparative Zoology asked us to sign on as "leaders" of a combined birding, archeological and remote-places expedition called " 'Round Britain," we figured it was as much challenge as invitation. Could two city-born and city-bred writers who didn't own a pair of hiking boots between them and knew almost nothing about birds, rocks, or neolithic society hold their own on an journey that seemed designed exclusively for those with a passion for identifying birds and tramping over long-abandoned, wind-whipped islands? This expedition wasn't bound for Wordsworth's Lake District, the Cotswolds, or any such bucolic area but instead  for Milton's "stormy Hebrides," surf-beaten islands and rocky pillars rising out of the North Atlantic. 

     Curious and open to the blandishments of the unexpected, we said yes. It turned out that our principal duties consisted of two sessions on how to keep a  travel journal by one of us, and a talk by the other on earlier visitors to these same regions, Samuel Johnson and James Boswell, for example. We also functioned as something like head counselors to the 18 passengers (of the ship's total of about 90) who had signed on under the auspices of the museum.

Word for Word: Military Gay Policy;When 'Don't Ask, Don't Tell' Means Do Ask and Do Tell All
BOOKEND;The Singer of Tales: On Joseph Brodsky
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Those who knew Joseph Brodsky were well aware that his heart disease was serious and that it would probably be the death of him, but because he always existed in his friends' minds not just as a person but as some kind of principle of indestructibility, it was difficult for them to admit that he was in danger.  The intensity and boldness of his genius plus the sheer exhilaration of being in his company kept you from thinking about the threat to his health; he had such valor and style, and lived at such a deliberate distance from self-pity and personal complaint, you were inclined to forget that he was as mortal as the next one. So his death in January at the age of 55 was all the more shocking and distressing. Having to speak of him in the past tense feels like an affront to grammar itself. 

      There was a wonderfully undoubting quality about Joseph, an intellectual readiness that was almost feral. Conversation attained immediate vertical takeoff and no deceleration was possible. Which is to say that he exemplified in life the very thing that he most cherished in poetry -- the capacity of language to go farther and faster than expected and thereby provide an escape from the limitations and the preoccupations of the self. Verbally, he had a lower boredom threshold than anyone I have ever known, forever punning, rhyming, veering off and homing in, unexpectedly raising the stakes or switching tracks. Words were a kind of high octane for him, and he loved to be propelled by them wherever they  took him. He also loved to put a spin on the words of others, whether by inspired misquotation or extravagant retort. Once, for example, when he was in Dublin and complaining about one of our rare heat waves, I suggested jokingly that he should take off for Iceland, and he replied in a flash, with a typical elevation and roguery, "But I could not tolerate the absence of meaning."

A ROYAL HAVEN IN PARIS
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Midnight on the Rue de la Bas tille. At the table next to ours outside the glass-domed Bofinger brasserie, two tanned blond lovers are fork-feeding each other oysters. Inside, amid lace and stained glass, sinuous Art Nouveau wood and zinc-topped counters, opera-goers in opera finery are polishing off the piles of crustaceans and the charcuterie served to them by orange-aproned waiters; outside, lean figures on motorized skateboards race by. 

     We can see, from our table, the gilded figure, the genie of Paris, atop the monument to liberty and to those who died in the uprisings of 1830 and 1848. And we can see the curved facade of the Bastille Opera, the brazenly white, brazenly modern, building of marble and granite that has been compared to both a ship in  full sail and (because of its decorative black-and-white tiles) to a public lav.

nytimes_articles.headline: Out of the Lab, A Revolution On the Farm
ON THE MAP;In Englewood, No Business Except Show Business
nytimes_articles.abstract:
When Erik Kessler, a retired musician, first came to the Actors' Fund Nursing Home in Englewood, he was convinced his life was over. Now, four years later, Mr. Kessler sits in the theatrical library that he helped put together for -- a  collection of scripts, playbills and books by and for the stage-struck. "I can do anything, and it's all because of this place," said Mr. Kessler, 72. "It's a harmonious place. I owe them my life, and I will always be grateful." 

     The library is a tangible sign of how show business permeates both past and present for residents of the Actors' Fund Retirement and Nursing Homes on West Hudson Avenue in Englewood, the only homes in the country run solely for those in the entertainment field -- actors, ushers, technicians and more.

Der Rosenkavalier' Has Yet to Yield Up Most of Its Secrets
nytimes_articles.abstract: THE OPERAS OF Richard Strauss have figured more prominently in the New York City Opera's repertory than might be expected, given their typical grand scale and extreme vocal demands. What the company has done is pick its spots. When it  presented "Der Rosenkavalier" in 1972, it enabled New Yorkers to hear that special artist Elisabeth Grummer, at the end of her career, as the Marschallin.  Now, on Saturday evening, the company offers that opera again, in a production by Jonathan Miller.

A Lapsed Man
nytimes_articles.abstract: HEARING VOICES By A. N. Wilson. 214 pp. New York: W.W. Norton & Company. $22.50.     THE novelist A. N. Wilson is a member of that vanishing species, the English man of letters: urbane, ironic, fluent, precise, felicitously erudite and, above all, pleasurably readable. The volumes of his Lampitt Papers series are narrated by Julian Ramsay, a failed novelist who has made a lifelong career as a character actor in a lowbrow radio soap opera. They cover the period from just before World War II to the present -- or, rather, a little beyond the present, since Julian's now is revealed in this fourth volume to be just over the threshold of the new millennium. "Hearing Voices," however, is largely concerned with events in his life during the 1960's.

Books in Brief: NONFICTION;Fractured Fairy Tales
nytimes_articles.abstract: Julie Taymor is one of the most remarkable theater artists of the last two decades, though she unfortunately remains known to only a relatively small group of cognoscenti. Like the works she creates, Ms. Taymor is not easily classifiable; she is a director, a designer, a mask and puppet maker, a choreographer, film maker and fabulist. She creates worlds of visual splendor and an almost childlike fantasy that, like good fairy tales, reveal grotesquerie and even horror just barely beneath the surface. Her work as a designer and director reflects her literal and artistic journeys through Sri Lanka, Indonesia, Bali, Japan and Paris and her apprenticeships with Joseph Chaikin, Herbert Blau and the Bread and Puppet Theater. This background has resulted in productions of Shakespeare, opera and original works in which the visual, the narrative and the symbolic are inseparable. Ms. Taymor integrates techniques and esthetics from a wide variety of theatrical cultures. But unlike  some contemporary directors who seem to plunder other cultures for inspiration only to come up with mere superficial borrowings, she creates a unique yet somehow familiar vocabulary that, as Eileen Blumenthal, a professor of theater arts at Rutgers University, says in her highly readable JULIE TAYMOR, PLAYING WITH FIRE: Theater, Opera, Film (Abrams, $49.50), is a "new theatrical organism." Ms. Taymor, who collaborated on the book with Ms. Blumenthal, describes each of the 15 productions she has designed or directed since her work in Bali in the mid-1970's. This book will be of interest to anyone who wonders where the magic, spirit and theatricality of the theater have gone in recent times, or to anyone who values the visual and creative imagination.   Arnold Aronson

HEADLESS BODY';Wrong Head
Interlude At Lake George
nytimes_articles.abstract:
OUR winter weekend at Lake George and Gore Mountain came about almost by accident. At a silent auction for the benefit of our local nature center, my husband, Rolf, had placed a bid for a long-weekend stay at a snow-covered cottage that had been seductively photographed in sight of a frozen Lake George. Among the area's attractions were cited nearness to a ski area new to us -- Gore Mountain -- and several fine country inns. Our bid captured the prize. 

      Because our trip involved coordinating schedules with another couple, we fixed on Presidents' Weekend last year. Given that the bareness of the slopes almost closed Northeastern ski areas in the winter of 1994-95, we were lucky to  have picked one of the few snowy interludes. And as insurance against the crowds, we decided to leave a day early, taking along our new cross-country skis.

A Man Who Dreams of Bubbles to Mars
nytimes_articles.abstract:
ASK most people what they think of bubbles and they'll recall the sweet summer days of youth, running through the grass, plastic bottle of soap solution in hand, dipping, then lifting the wand to the lips to blow, watching and giggling as one by one the shiny spheres floated, then burst with a splash. For  most people, that's pretty much the end of bubbles. 

     But ask James Ossi of Darien about bubbles and he'll tell you about science,  art, Mars, failure, his father, a young man's suicide, architecture, success and dreams.

EARNING IT;The Virtual Office Bumps Into Some Very Real Limits
nytimes_articles.abstract:
FOR seven years, Judith S. Lederman worked out of her home and employed six other consultants who worked from their homes, too. But whatever its benefits, this virtual office frequently left her frustrated. "I was getting a level of work that was unprofessional, and I was unable to monitor it," she said. "The projects were floundering." 

     It was the cheesecake, though, that really got Ms. Lederman's goat. One of the consultants she had retained failed to meet a deadline, and when Ms. Lederman asked her about it, the woman said that a friend had come over and brought a cheesecake. Apparently, the work had been put on hold while the two enjoyed some dessert.

SOAPBOX;Christopher and Me
nytimes_articles.abstract:
One of the unexpected joys of moving back to Queens from Greenwich Village three years ago to care for my ailing mother was the friendship of a little East Indian boy who lives next door. Christopher, 4, has shiny black hair in a Beatles cut, enormous brown eyes and a keen sense of humor. 

      It was so hot that first summer that we used to leave our door propped open  to catch a breeze. Christopher began dropping in regularly. He soon invented a game in which my mother, sitting in her chair with her back toward us, became the Scary Monster, and I, his magic shield.

PLAYWRIGHTS AND TV;Theater Is Irrelevant?
New in High School: Lessons in Trust
nytimes_articles.abstract:
IN a traditional high school physical education class, climbing the walls of  the gymnasium would not be tolerated. Neither would jumping off bleachers or jumping off telephone poles. Most teachers would not allow boys and girls to hold hands or hug each other in class, either. 

     But in the Project Adventure physical education classes at Sachem High School North in Lake Ronkonkoma, such activities are not only accepted, but also encouraged. By the end of the school year, the teachers hope, students will scale a 15-foot wall and jump off a 60-foot-high telephone pole while attached to a pulley system.

Their hands, some hardened by the years of confining housework they did in the dry Yemeni countryside, are decorated with henna inks that take hours to apply but are a standard beautifier. 

     The orange-colored fingers now clasp pencils in a classroom in the Nathan Hale Middle School as Arabic-speaking women are taught English. The task has opened them up to realities they never knew existed.

PRACTICAL TRAVELER;Quilt-Making Or Bookbinding?
nytimes_articles.abstract:
YOU know the idea of learning vacations is gaining ground when it gets the Disney stamp of approval. The Disney Institute, which opened last month as a Walt Disney World resort in Lake Buena Vista, Fla., is a theme park for adults who want to dabble in a variety of areas from culinary arts to animation techniques. The institute offers two- and three-hour programs including how to shoot better home videos, collect antiques or talk like Mickey Mouse. Until May  18, three-night packages, including lodging but not meals, cost $349 a person; after May 18, the packages cost from $518 to $630 a person; (800) 282-9282, fax  (407) 939-4898. 

     Dan Higgins, a spokesman for the institute, emphasizes that "we're not a place to come and learn and become more proficient at something; it's a vacation experience." But many other programs take a more intensive approach.

wiki_events.headline: A derailed train in Weyauwega, Wisconsin (USA) causes the emergency evacuation of 2,300 people for 16 days.

pages.title: Weyauwega, Wisconsin, derailment
pages.abstract: The Weyauwega derailment was a railroad accident that occurred in Weyauwega, Wisconsin, United States, in the early morning hours of March 4, 1996. The derailed train was carrying a large quantity of hazardous material, which immediately caught fire. The fire, which involved the train cars and an adjacent feed mill, burned for more than two weeks after the actual derailment, resulting in the emergency evacuation of 2,300 people for 18 days, including the entire city of Weyauwega, with about 1,700 evacuees.

pages.title: Emergency evacuation
pages.description: Urgent removal of people from an area of imminent or ongoing threat
pages.abstract: An emergency evacuation is an immediate egress or escape of people away from an area that contains an imminent threat, an ongoing threat or a hazard to lives or property.
